Yes, when I smoke it, the heavy feeling goes away in a few minutes and I feel normal again. But then it comes back after some time

Usually I wake up, brush my teeth, make tea, sit outside for a bit, and then smoke the first bidi with the tea. After that I feel okay to start the day
I like the tea and bidi together, it feels relaxing. Giving it up sounds difficult, maybe I can try something else but I don't know what.
It's the hot tea in the morning air, sitting quietly outside — it just makes everything feel peaceful before the day starts. The bidi part is part of that peace, I guess
I guess I would still feel peaceful for a bit with just the tea and the quiet, but after some time the heavy head would probably come back anyway. It's hard to imagine not having the bidi there
It's annoying when the heavy head comes back so fast. I wish it didn't happen, but the bidi is the only thing that fixes it quickly. I don't really want to think about going without it right now
I don't know what drives it — I just want the heavy head to go away fast. Can we talk about something else? Talking about this makes me feel more irritated
When it comes back, my mind just keeps thinking 'I need a bidi now' over and over. It's like my brain won't shut up until I smoke
Yes, sometimes in the evening when I'm alone after work, I feel bored and reach for a bidi to pass the time. It's different from the morning one
just in the evening when i feel bored and noting to do , i smoke a bidi.
When I feel bored and have nothing to do, I just sit there, maybe look at my phone or stare at the wall, and then I light a bidi to have something to do with my hands
It's boring and uncomfortable. I feel restless, like time is dragging. Smoking gives me something to focus on for a few minutes

I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.
The doctor kept telling me about my breathing problems and said it would get worse. I get a lot of coughing, especially in the cold weather. I'm worried about my health.
The coughing. It's hard to work in the fields when I'm coughing all the time. It hurts and it's hard to breathe.
Just being able to work without getting tired so fast. And being able to breathe properly. That would be a big change.
I think I should just try to reduce my smoking some more. From 3 or 4 bidis a day, maybe I can try to go to 2 or 1.
If I can find something else to do in the morning when I wake up, like drinking tea or going for a walk. Maybe that would help me not think about smoking as much.
I need to remind myself to do it every day. Maybe I can tell my wife to remind me too, if she sees me reaching for a bidi.
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
I'd save some money, I guess. And maybe my wife wouldn't yell at me so much about spending too much on bidis. But what if I fail? I'll just feel bad, and the guys on site will still be smoking.
I suppose I could try to limit myself to just one packet a day while I'm working on this project. That way, even if I don't quit completely, at least I'll be saving some money.
My coworkers are always offering me a bidi, saying 'have one, yaar'. When we're on break or after lunch. If they're smoking, it's hard for me to say no.
I don't know, maybe just say "no thanks, I'm cutting down" or something? I don't want to lie to them, but I also don't want to give in to the bidi.
"No thanks, I'm cutting down." (pausing) Yeah, that sounds okay, I guess.
I think they'd be more annoyed that I'm quitting, not understanding why I'm doing it. They'd just think I'm being crazy or weak.

I think it was just how normal he was. He didn't pretend to be someone he wasn't, and he didn't make a big show of it. He just did it, and it made a big difference. That's what I want to be like, I guess. Just making a change, bit by bit.

motivation_reason: live long enough to see her grow up
don't want to leave her alone without a father

past_strategies.strategy: being strong enough to put it down
thinking about being a healthier father

session_notes.note: I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.
My daughter, she brought me here. She's concerned about my health, says I get out of breath easily when it's cold outside. And I guess she wants me to be healthier.

It means... I want to live long enough to see her grow up, married with kids of her own. My wife and I always talked about that. Now it's just me, and I don't want to leave her alone without a father.
It feels... empty. Like a part of me is already gone. My wife, she was the one who always made me feel needed. Now, without her, I just feel... lost.

(pausing, looking down) She was always there for me, supported me in everything. We'd sit together in the evenings, and she'd listen to me talk about my day. She had a way of making me feel like I was the only person in the world. I don't feel that way anymore, since she's... gone. (pausing again, collecting thoughts) I'm just trying to make it through each day, to be honest.

(thoughtfully) Yeah... I think it was the other night, around 3 AM. I woke up, couldn't sleep, and I just started thinking about my wife. I felt so alone, and I picked up a bidi without even realizing it. But I was able to put it down, after a while. My daughter's been reminding me to take care of myself, so I guess that helped.

(pauses, reflecting) I don't know... probably I would've lit it, and who knows how long I would've smoked. Maybe I would've gotten caught up in the whole thing again. It's hard to stop when you've been doing it for so long, and it feels like a crutch sometimes. But that time, I guess I was able to be strong enough to put it down.

(sighs) It's like, when I'm feeling down or anxious, or just bored like I am now, smoking seems like the easiest thing to do. It's like a habit, a reflex. I don't really think about it, I just do it. And when I'm trying to quit, I feel like I'm fighting that reflex, that habit. It's hard to shake it off, especially when it feels like it's just there, waiting for me to need it again.

(thinks for a moment) Yeah... today, actually. I was sitting here, feeling a bit restless, and I started thinking about how nice it would be to have a bidi right now. I know I've been trying to cut back, but it felt so tempting just then. But I didn't actually get up and smoke, because I was here in this chair, talking to you.

(thinks for a moment) I guess I just sat here and talked to you. It took my mind off of it, a bit. And I was thinking about what you said earlier, about being a healthier father for my daughter. That helped too.

(thinks for a moment) Yeah... I could try taking a walk, or reading a book. My daughter likes to send me articles and stories on my phone, so maybe I could read something like that. Or I could try calling her, talking to her about my day. That usually helps me feel a bit better.

(thinks for a moment) I think mornings would be tough, but maybe right after our sessions like this? You know, when I'm still feeling a bit more engaged, more focused. Maybe if I can make a plan with you to do something after our sessions, like go for a walk or something, I can use that as a reason to stay on track.
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.

It's just... after a rough day at school, or an argument with my wife about our son... I feel trapped, you know? And then I light a cigarette to calm down, to think clearly. It's like it helps me relax, and then I can deal with the situation better. But I know that's not right.

I used to like playing guitar, but I haven't picked it up in years. And running, I used to run every morning, but that stopped too. It's hard to think of anything else that really helps me calm down like smoking does.

I don't know... It's hard to get back into things after such a long time. And I'm not sure if it would help with the cravings when I'm feeling really stressed or angry. Smoking's just so... automatic, you know?
I guess... I could try. But only if it works, okay? I don't want to waste my time if it's not going to help with the cravings. And what if I still want to smoke after 10-15 minutes?

I suppose if I can get through that time without feeling like I'm going to lose control and smoke, that would be a success. And if it makes me feel a little bit better, not as angry... that would be good too.
I don't know... A decent cup of coffee maybe? Or watching a funny video for a bit? Something to take my mind off things and give me a little boost.

She could be my accountability partner, I guess. Just check in with me and see how I'm doing, remind me of my goals. And maybe she could help me come up with more ideas for things to do when I'm feeling stressed or angry. That would be helpful.

I don't know... I guess I'd feel a little ashamed, I suppose. Like a failure or something. That I didn't do better, that I couldn't control my cravings. But I guess that's just part of getting better, right?

Yeah, I think it is. I always used to think that I couldn't quit because I was a failure, because I wasn't strong enough or whatever. But this time... this time I'm thinking that it's not about being strong or weak, it's just about figuring it out, you know? It's not a failure if I slip up, it's just a chance to learn and try again.

past_strategies.strategy: going to the back of the shop and straightening up the shelves

session_notes.note: Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.

My wife, actually. She's been on my case for years, saying I'm going to get sick and die if I don't stop. But I always just tell her not to worry, that I'll live longer than her anyway. (chuckles) But I guess she finally got me tired of arguing about it, so now I'm here.

I guess that's fair. I mean, I don't feel like quitting for myself, but if it makes her happy, I'm willing to give it a shot. But if it's too hard, I'm probably going to slip up and go back to smoking. That's just what I do.

Honestly, I'm not really sure. I guess I've always thought it was just a harmless habit, like drinking a cup of tea. But I suppose I've noticed I'm getting a bit short of breath sometimes, when I'm working in the shop and carrying heavy bags. Maybe being smoke-free would help with that? But it's not like it's a big deal or anything.

Yeah, like after tea in the morning. That's usually when I have a cigarette. But I guess I could replace it with... I don't know, chewing gum or something. That's what my cousin does, I think. Or maybe just taking a walk outside for a minute. That'd be okay, I suppose.

I don't know... it sounds like a bit of a fuss. But, I guess it couldn't hurt to try. Just do something, you know? Like... like going to the back of the shop and straightening up the shelves or something. Yeah, that could work. Not too hard, not too weird.

I'm not sure... I mean, my wife's been on my case about this for years, like I said. But, I suppose if I told her I'm actually trying to quit, she'd feel like she'd won or something. And, you know, it might be good to have someone to tell, I guess. Yeah, maybe I'll mention it to her.

I don't know... I guess she'd probably be happy for me, or relieved or something. And it's true, sharing it with her might help me stay on track, because I'd feel like I'm doing it for myself this time, not just for her. Yeah, maybe that's not such a bad idea after all.

Yeah, I guess I will. If she's invested in this, it'll be easier for me to stay on track, too. And it's not like I'm doing it for her, exactly... I mean, I am, but it's also like we're working together, you know? Yeah, that's a good feeling.

motivation_reason: hard to think about cravings as tasks
make it through this morning without smoking
feeling stuck and powerless over smoking

session_notes.note: I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.

I used nicotine gum for a while, it helped to curb the cravings a bit, but I still ended up smoking. I remember thinking it was okay to have a cigarette if I was using the gum, but that only made it worse.

Honestly, I don't know. I just feel really overwhelmed when I'm thinking about quitting and dealing with cravings at the same time. It's like, I'm trying to quit smoking, but I'm also fighting this monster in my head telling me to just have one more cigarette.

I don't know... I guess it would be good to take it one step at a time, like with work. But it's hard to think about cravings as tasks, you know? It's like, when I'm craving a cigarette, I just want to smoke, not break it down into something I can manage.

There was one time... I was getting ready for a meeting, and I had a really strong craving to smoke, but I didn't have time to step outside. So I just... just took a few deep breaths and waited until the meeting was over. It wasn't long, but it was something. I think what helped was just knowing that I could put it off for a little while, you know? It didn't make the craving go away, but it... it gave me a tiny bit of control, I guess.

I think I might be able to do something similar, like... taking a short walk around the house or doing a quick stretch to distract myself. But it's hard because, like you said, morning cravings are usually the worst for me. And it's always the same routine, getting ready for work, making breakfast... it's like, I'm so used to having a cigarette right after I eat.

I can try it, I guess. It's not like I have anything to lose, right? But it's hard because my brain is already racing with all the things I need to do that morning... I'm not sure if I can actually remember to take a walk or stretch before I start my routine.

It's... it's actually kind of hopeful, I guess. I mean, it's not like I'm trying to quit cold turkey or anything, I'm just trying to make a small change. And it's not like it's going to solve everything, but it's something, you know? It's like, maybe I can make it through this morning without smoking, and that's a start, right?

Yeah, it does. I mean, I've been feeling so stuck and powerless over my smoking for a long time, and just thinking about taking that one step - taking a few deep breaths while I brush my teeth - feels like I'm taking back some control. It feels like, maybe I'm not totally powerless after all.

motivation_reason: trying to do something good for myself

session_notes.note: It's just...I don't know how people deal without it. It's always been there, you know? Like, I wake up, I have a cigarette. What's the point of quitting if it's going to make me feel miserable?

Well, playing cricket with my friends, I guess. We always take a break and smoke, but if I quit maybe we could just drink tea or something. It might be kinda cool to just chill with them without needing a cigarette.

Maybe it's not just about me and my addiction, but about all of us doing something together. It'd be a team effort, you know? We could all try to quit together, or at least find new ways to hang out. That might make it easier.

I don't know, it's just that with these activities, I feel like we'd be doing something together, you know? We'd be having a good time, and it wouldn't be just about smoking anymore. Smoking's just...there, it's just something we do, but with these activities, it feels like we'd actually be enjoying each other's company.

I'll tell him, I'll be honest with him, that I'm trying to quit. He might not care, I don't know, but at least I'll be upfront about it. And if he gets upset or whatever, that's not my problem, I'm trying to do something good for myself.

motivation_reason: doing something I've been wanting to do for a while
it's something I can wrap my head around

session_notes.note: I quit for about 3 months last year. Then a really bad week at work happened and I started again. Now I'm back to 15 a day.

I don't know, it just seemed like everything was crashing down at once and I needed a way to cope. Maybe it was just the stress and the pressure. I don't know if there's a way to avoid it, but I think I need a better plan for when that happens. What do you think I could do differently?

I guess I started walking to work instead of driving, and I found some yoga classes online that I could follow at home. I also made a little habit of drinking tea in the morning, instead of coffee. It just gave me a feeling of calm, you know? But I stopped doing all that when I started smoking again. I'm not sure how to make sure I keep doing it this time.

Yeah... it sounds nice, I guess. Like, if I'm feeling more put together, I might not be as tempted to smoke when things get crazy. And maybe I'll be able to handle stressful meetings better, without falling back into old habits. But, I don't know... it's still just a feeling, and it's easy to say that it'll happen, but it's hard to actually make it happen.

I guess I just feel like I've been down this road before, and it's easy to get all excited about the idea of quitting, but it's hard to actually stick to it. Like, I said it worked for 3 months last time, but that was just a fluke, right? This time will be the same, and I'll just end up falling back into my old habits.

I guess that's part of it. But I think what's really bothering me is that I feel like I let myself down, you know? I proved to myself that I could quit, and then I went and failed again. It's hard to live with that, and it makes me feel like maybe I'm just not cut out for this.

I guess I just did it. I mean, I woke up every morning and didn't reach for a cigarette. And I went to those yoga classes and walked to work. I actually did it, even if it wasn't for forever. And that's something, right?

I don't know... maybe just take a minute to think about it. Like, when I first quit and it was going well, I'd treat myself to a coffee at that new cafe down the street. It was a little thing, but it felt like a reward. Maybe I could do that again, or something like that.

That's not a bad idea. A week or two might be long enough for me to feel like I'm really making progress. Maybe I could plan a hike or something, since I like walking. That way, I've got something to look forward to, and it's not just about quitting smoking, but about doing something I enjoy.

I guess it's just that planning a hike is something I can actually get excited about, you know? It's something that's not just about quitting a bad habit, but about doing something I've been wanting to do for a while. And it's tangible - I can pick a place, check the weather, make a plan... it's something I can wrap my head around. Quitting smoking is just... quitting smoking. It's not as concrete.
I smoke because it calms me down when I feel anxious. I know it makes things worse but I can't stop.

It's like my heart's racing and I get these tight sensations in my chest. And then smoking somehow makes that go away, like a quick fix. As for quitting, I don't know... it just feels too hard. My partner and friends all smoke, and it's like they'd be quitting too if I did.

I've tried deep breathing, but it's hard to remember to do it when I'm in the middle of a panic. And exercise? It sounds good, but it's not like I can just go for a run when I'm feeling overwhelmed... that would make it worse.

I don't know... That sounds like it might help, but it's not like I can just take a walk when I'm in the middle of a project or something. And what if I'm out with friends and I feel anxious? It's hard to just get up and leave.
I guess that could work... But it's hard to do anything if I'm at a social event and everyone's around me. And what if it doesn't even help? I don't want to feel like a failure in front of people.

Yeah, that might be okay... I could ask my sister to come with me to events or stuff, she's always been really supportive. But what if she's smoking too? It's hard to know if she'd be supportive if she's smoking alongside everyone else.
I could talk to her about how it would help me if she was there with me when I feel like smoking, and how I'm trying to quit for my own health. Maybe she wouldn't have to quit to support me.
I think I mentioned quitting to her before, but it was a long time ago, after I tried cutting down and failed. I don't think I've really talked about it with her since.

Maybe I'm more serious about quitting this time, and I've thought about it more. And I'm not just trying to quit because someone told me to, it's more because I'm worried about my health and... and I don't know, feeling like I'm in control, I guess.

I guess it's when I'm under a lot of pressure to meet a deadline or when I'm in a noisy crowded place, like a party or a concert. When I'm smoking, it feels like it's helping me cope with all that overwhelm, but really it's just masking it. Quitting would mean facing that anxiety head-on, and I'm not sure if I'm ready for that.

past_strategies.strategy: try to handle situation without smoking

session_notes.note: I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.

It was just... the guy I was with, we'd been friends since high school, and it was like slipping back into an old habit. Plus, we were all laughing and joking, and smoking seemed like part of the moment. But now that I think about it, we were all actually pretty loud and rowdy, but I'm the one who lit up.

I don't know, it's not really about him, I think. It's just that I've always used smoking as a way to fit in with guys, or at least that's what I tell myself. Like, when we're all hanging out, drinking, and it's late, it's just what guys do, you know? I feel like if I don't smoke, I'm not part of that scene.

I guess it would be weird at first, but maybe it would actually be okay. Maybe we'd find other stuff to do, like play some music or play video games, and it would be just as fun without the smoking. Or maybe we'd just hang out and talk without needing to smoke. I don't know, it feels like a big change, but I guess it's possible.

I think a good reward would be to do something I've been wanting to do, but never make time for, like going to a concert or trying out a new restaurant. Something that's just for me, and has nothing to do with smoking or hanging out with friends.

I'll take a step back, breathe, and remind myself why I'm quitting. I'll also try to figure out what triggered it, like if I was stressed or bored, and see if there's another way to handle that situation without smoking. And if it happens again, I'll try to be kind to myself and not give up. One slip-up doesn't mean I've failed, it just means I need to try harder next time.

I think the hardest situation for me will be when I'm out with friends on weekends, especially if we're drinking. That's when I associate smoking with having fun and being part of the group. I'll need to figure out ways to stay present and aware of my choices, like taking deep breaths and distracting myself with the game or conversation. Maybe we can even set up a signal with my friend that I'll use if I feel like I'm about to slip up, like a text message or a discreet knock on the table, so he can give me a boost of encouragement.

That's a tough one. I might try to find a trusted friend to hang out with in a different part of the bar, so I'm not surrounded by everyone else smoking. Or, if that's not possible, I'll try to focus on what I'm doing, whether it's playing a game on my phone, listening to music, or just enjoying the conversation. I'll also remind myself why I'm quitting and what I'm trying to achieve, to keep my motivation strong.

motivation_reason: being a role model for my daughter
be a good role model for her
want to be around for her growing up
don't want to deal with breathing problems when I'm older

past_strategies.strategy: wrote down all my reasons for quitting
putting a paper on my fridge

session_notes.note: I've tried to quit 3 times. The longest I went was 3 months. But I just can't seem to stay quit. My daughter is growing up and I don't want her to see me like this.

Honestly, I don't know if it was just a fluke or what. I was really focused then - I think just thinking about being a role model for my daughter made a big difference. I also wrote down all my reasons for quitting and past that paper on my fridge. But when things got stressful at work, I just fell back into old habits.
That sounds like a good idea, I guess. I mean, thinking ahead might help. But what if I forget it when I'm in the middle of a meeting and I just need something to calm me down?

Yeah, I used to take deep breaths, but I haven't done that in a while. And going for a walk is hard when I'm in the middle of a meeting or driving to work. I've actually tried chewing gum sometimes, but that just leads to me looking for a cigarette instead.

I think it would be the list of reasons why I want to quit, to be honest. It's easy to get caught up in the stress and just want a cigarette, but if I remind myself of why I'm doing this, it might help. Like, I want to be healthy for my daughter and I want to be a good role model for her. That kind of thing.
.. I guess it couldn't hurt. What if I really do forget, though? Can I take a piece of paper and write down my reasons for quitting right now?

Okay, yeah... I'll write them down. *pauses to write down reasons* Okay, I've got "be healthy for my daughter", "be a good role model", "want to be around for her growing up", "don't want to deal with breathing problems when I'm older"... *pauses again* You know, just writing this down makes me feel a little better, I guess.
Okay, yeah... I'll try it. I'll carry it with me for the next day or two and see if it makes a difference. But what if it's just a lot to carry around?

motivation_reason: be stronger for the long run

session_notes.note: I chew gutkha, not cigarettes. About 8 to 10 pouches a day for the last 7 years. My mouth has developed some kind of white patch and the doctor is worried.

It's easy to say I'll reduce it but honestly, I don't know how. I'm a driver, I spend most of my day in the rickshaw, waiting at red lights, and gutkha helps pass the time. It's become a habit, and without it, I'll just be sitting idle, doing nothing.

I'm a driver, I'm always thinking about money. But I guess I could listen to music or podcasts or something. I've seen some of my friends do that, but I've never really bothered to try it. What if I just got bored after a few minutes and started chewing gutkha again?
I don't know... we've got distance between us now, he's busy with his family... I don't want to bother him with this gutkha thing. What if he just tells me to stop chewing it and leaves it at that?

I guess I could just ask him how his family is doing, and maybe casually mention that I'm trying to give up gutkha. That way, if he doesn't want to talk about it, I can just change the subject back to his family.

I think it would help a lot... it would be good to know that someone cares about my health, you know? And maybe it would even give me the motivation to actually follow through with quitting gutkha. It's not just about the white patch in my mouth, it's about feeling like I'm taking care of myself...

I think the first step would be to actually talk to my brother, like I said. After that, maybe I can try to reduce the number of pouches I chew each day, start with one or two less and see how it goes. And then... I don't know, maybe try to find some other way to occupy myself at the red lights. Listen to music or something. Yeah, that's a good start.

If he could just call or message me every day, ask how it's going, that would be a big help. Just knowing that I've got to report back to him would keep me accountable, you know? Or maybe he could even ask me to send him a picture of myself not chewing gutkha, just to prove I'm being honest. That would be funny, and it would also remind me of what I'm trying to do.

That's the scary part... I don't know if I'd be honest with him right away. I might try to downplay it, say it was just one little slip-up, and then I'll be back on track. But deep down, I know I'd be beating myself up over it, and it would be harder to stop the next time. Maybe it's better to just be honest with him from the start, even if it's hard. He'll understand, and it might even make me stronger for the long run.

I think I'd need to feel like he's not going to judge me too harshly, you know? Like, he'll understand that it's just a slip-up and it doesn't mean I'm a total failure or anything. Maybe if he could just remind me that quitting gutkha is a journey, not a destination, and that it's okay to make mistakes along the way. That would help me feel more comfortable being honest about it.

I could just tell him how I'm feeling, that I'm worried about being judged and stuff. Maybe I could even say something like, "Hey, if I slip up and chew gutkha, I'll be honest with you about it, but please don't be too hard on me, okay?" That way, we'll be on the same page and he'll know how I'm feeling.
I started smoking 2 years ago in college. My friends all do it. I'm on the cricket team and my stamina is getting really bad now.
I don't know... they'll probably think I'm weak or something. They smoke all the time and they're not really into sports like I am.
Yeah, I guess so. But what if I'm letting the team down? If I quit smoking, will we still be the same strong team? Coach already noticed my performance is off...
I don't know... I don't feel like I'm holding back on purpose or anything. It's just... sometimes during games, I get a bit winded and I know I shouldn't, but it's like my body just can't keep up.

I remember that time I had to study for a really hard exam and I would take breaks to run up and down the stairs in my apartment building. It helped me clear my head and I felt more focused afterwards. Maybe something like that could work for me with smoking...
Yeah, maybe... like, instead of smoking, I could take a short walk outside or something. But what if I need it before a big game and I'm really nervous?

I guess it's worth a shot... I mean, it's not like I'm getting any younger, and if quitting smoking will help me stay on the team, it's worth trying some other things to help with the stress, like the stress ball and deep breathing.

I think it's just getting started, you know? If I'm in a group of friends who are all smoking, it's hard to suddenly pull out a stress ball and start breathing into it. They'll think I'm crazy or something.

Actually, our team captain is really into fitness and healthy living. He's always talking about how it helps his game. Maybe I could talk to him about it, see if he'd be willing to support me and help me get through this.

I'd probably just be honest with him about how I'm feeling and why I want to quit. I'd ask if he thinks it would benefit my game if I stopped smoking, and if he's okay with me being a little different for a while. I guess I just hope he'll be understanding and maybe even offer some advice or encouragement.

It would mean a lot if he said yes. I think it would show me that he's invested in my success, not just as a player, but as a person too. And it would make it way easier to stay on track if I knew he was behind me.
My son brought me here. I smoke 4-5 bidis a day and eat paan. I've been doing this for 30 years. I don't think I can stop at this age.
It's just that everyone I know who's quit, they've done it when they were younger. I don't know, maybe it's just that I'm used to it. And my health is fine, mostly. My son is worried, though.

He's been saying that I'm an example to him and his children, that they look up to me. He wants me to live long enough to see them grow up. He's worried that if I keep smoking, I might not be around for them.
(shrugging) I don't know, it's just that I've been smoking for 30 years. It's just who I am, I guess. And I've tried before, but I started again. It feels like I'm stuck in a cycle.
(hesitating) It's hard to admit, but it feels like I'm not in control. Like I'm just going through the motions. But it's also... it's also hard to break.
(looking down, slightly interested) A walk, you say? That might be okay, I guess. I do like walking when I'm out in the fields helping with the vegetables.
(thinking) I suppose I would still want a paan, but maybe it wouldn't be as big of a deal if I was walking. And I might feel a bit more... refreshed, maybe.
(hesitating) I don't know... maybe if I had something else to do with my hands, like stitching or something. That might take my mind off paan.
(smiling slightly) I could write in the afternoon, when I usually rest. That way, I'd have something to look forward to. And maybe I could even write about my health and how I'm trying to quit.

(thinking) I guess I could try to find other things to do, like going for a walk or helping with the household chores. Or... I could call one of my daughters and talk to her. That usually makes me feel better.

past_strategies.strategy: saying no to friends and family

session_notes.note: I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.

It's like, when I'm drinking, I feel like I need a cigarette to calm down. And I know it's a bad habit, but I've been doing it for so long, it just feels like a part of the thing. If I'm going to stop smoking, I feel like I need to quit drinking too, or at least cut back on it a lot. But that's a lot to ask, you know?

I guess I feel like I've tried quitting smoking before, and it didn't work out. If I'm just quitting smoking and not drinking, I don't know if it'll make a difference, you know? I don't want to try something and then fail, especially since I know my wife is counting on me to quit.

Yeah, maybe. I do feel like I need to get it right this time, or it's all for nothing. But if I'm being honest, I'm also a little scared of not being perfect. If I slip up, will it be a total failure?

I guess it's close. My wife is really worried about my health, and I don't want to let her down. But at the same time, the thought of quitting and then smoking again... it's just really hard to shake that feeling that I'll never be able to do it for good.

It would mean a lot to me, and to her too, I think. She's been asking me to quit for years, and it would be nice to finally be able to do it and feel like I'm doing something for her, for us. I don't want to keep disappointing her. But at the same time, I don't want to put so much pressure on myself that I end up giving up before I even start.

Yeah, I think she'd be really proud of me, even if I didn't quit completely. She'd see that I'm trying, and that I'm not giving up. And maybe that's what I need to focus on - not being perfect, but just trying my best.

I could try reducing the number of cigarettes I smoke on the weekends, or just on drinking days. Or maybe I could start paying more attention to when I'm reaching for a cigarette and see if there's something else I can do instead. It's not like I have to quit tomorrow, or even this week.

I don't know. I guess I'd just have to say no. It's not that hard, is it? I mean, I've said no to my friends and family when they've offered me a cigarette before. It's just like any other habit, right?

Honestly, it's harder when it's just me and the cigarette. There's no one to answer to, no one to impress. It's just me and my own habits. But I guess that's when I need to focus on why I'm trying to quit in the first place, and why it's worth it. My wife, my health... that's what I need to keep in mind.

I think I'll write it down, put it somewhere I can see it every day. Like on my phone or on a piece of paper on my fridge. So when I'm having a tough moment, I can just look and remember why I'm doing this. And maybe I'll also talk to my wife about it, ask her to remind me too. That way, I'll have her support, and it'll be harder to give up.
I've been smoking for about 2 years. My husband and I are planning to have a baby soon. I know I need to stop.

He's really supportive – he never smoked in the first place, so he's always been against it. He's been saying we should do it together, but I'm not sure if he really understands what it takes to quit.

If we could make a plan together, like set a specific date for me to quit, and he could, you know, help me stick to it – that would be great. Maybe he could also help me find ways to manage stress at home instead of reaching for a cigarette. That would be really helpful.

We could try going for walks together, or doing some light exercise, like yoga. Or he could just be there to talk to me, listen to me when I'm stressed. Sometimes just knowing I have someone to talk to can make a big difference.

No, we've done yoga a few times, but not regularly. And we've gone for walks, but not as much as we should. If we make quitting a priority, I'm sure we can make time for it. It's just...getting started will be tough, I think.

I guess we could start by setting a timeline, like you said, and picking a quit date. Once we have a specific date in mind, it'll feel more real, and we can start making plans for how to handle cravings and stress around that time.

I think we should aim to stop smoking as soon as possible, but not so soon that it's unrealistic. Maybe we could start by trying to reduce the number of cigarettes I smoke each day, and then gradually cut back until I'm not smoking at all. My husband could check in with me every day to see how I'm doing, and we could set small goals for me to reach each week.

I could start by identifying specific situations that trigger me to want to smoke, like during work breaks or right after dinner. And then we could come up with alternative behaviors to do in those situations, like going for a walk or eating a healthy snack. My husband could also remind me to take deep breaths when I'm feeling stressed or craving a cigarette.

I could keep a journal or log to track when I crave a cigarette or want to smoke, and what I'm doing at the time. That way, I can identify patterns and see if there are certain things that I can avoid or do differently to prevent cravings. My husband and I could also set reminders on our phones to take breaks and practice relaxation techniques, like deep breathing or meditation. And I could try to find a supportive friend or colleague who can check in with me during the day to see how I'm doing.

We could celebrate small milestones with things like going out for a nice dinner, seeing a movie together, or taking a relaxing bath. For bigger milestones, like a month without smoking, we could do something bigger like a weekend getaway or a fun outdoor activity. Having something to look forward to would make it feel like it's worth sticking to the plan.

For staying smoke-free for a day, my husband said we could go to a coffee shop together and get my favorite pastry. And for a week, I think we could go see a concert or a comedy show, something fun to take our minds off things. Having those rewards in mind will definitely help me get through the tough days.
I work from home and since 2020 my smoking has more than doubled. I used to smoke 5 a day and now it's 18-20.

To be honest, I think it's just boredom and cabin fever. When I'm in an office, there are more distractions and I feel like I'm moving around more. At home, it's easy to get stuck on a task and just keep smoking, thinking I need a break, but it's more like procrastination.

Well, I've tried telling myself to take breaks, but it doesn't always work. Maybe I could set some rules, like getting up every hour to grab a glass of water or something? Or maybe I could do some actual exercise, like a quick run or some yoga, but that feels harder to fit in when I'm working on a deadline.
That sounds like a more realistic approach, I guess. I've tried making New Year's resolutions before, but it never sticks. If I can start small and make it a habit, that might be more manageable.

I think I'd feel a bit more focused and refreshed, maybe even less likely to reach for a cigarette when I'm feeling anxious or bored. It's funny, my wife is pregnant right now, and I've been trying to do things better for her and the baby, but I haven't really been doing much for myself. Maybe this is a step in the right direction.

I guess I could remind myself that my wife is pregnant and that's a big motivator for me to be healthier. I could write it down somewhere, like a sticky note on my desk, and look at it every time I'm feeling tempted to smoke or procrastinate. That way, I'll be thinking about why I want to quit and get healthy, and not just about beating a habit.
Something like "Be healthy for baby" or "New habit, new life". That way I'll see it every time I'm working from home and it'll remind me of what's at stake.

I guess I could try deep breathing exercises or something, instead of reaching for a cigarette. And if that doesn't work, maybe I can pick up the phone and call my wife, just to talk to her for a bit. That usually helps me calm down.

I don't know... I guess I'll just have to be kinder to myself, maybe acknowledge that it's okay to slip up and that it doesn't mean I've failed completely. And then I can try to figure out what went wrong and how to fix it next time.
I used to smoke one and a half bundles of bidis a day. That's about 25. Now I'm down to 2 or 3. My doctor says I should stop completely.
To be honest, it was my granddaughter, she's always telling me how unhealthy smoking is, and I thought if I cut back, it would make her happy, and also maybe improve my health a bit.

If I stopped completely, I think I'll be able to do things with her that I'm not doing now, like taking her on long walks, playing with her. She's always telling me I should spend more quality time with her.
It feels...guilty, and also a bit worried that I might not have as much time as I want with her. Her parents might get worried about my health too.
I think if I'm being honest, it's more that her parents might be disappointed. I don't want to let them down, and I think they're already a bit worried, I can see it in their faces.

If I could stop smoking completely, it would mean a lot to me that her parents trust me to take care of myself. We'd be able to talk about this like normal, instead of them being worried all the time.
One situation is when I wake up in the morning and feel like having a bidi. It's been my morning habit for years. I'm not sure how to break that habit.
It might make me feel more refreshed, and a bit more energetic. Maybe that would make the thought of having a bidi feel less appealing.
That would feel great. It would be proof to myself that I can do it, and I think it would give me more confidence to keep going.

motivation_reason: need for a break from the stress of the road

session_notes.note: I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. I calculate I spend about 4000 rupees a month on cigarettes. My family is struggling and I feel guilty about it.

It makes me feel frustrated. I think about how much money that is, and how much we could use it for the kids' school fees. It's hard to justify it, but I've been doing it for so long now, it's just something I do when I'm stressed or alone during deliveries.

I could use that frustration to motivate me to quit, I guess. If I could just find a way to avoid smoking when I'm feeling stressed or alone, I might be able to cut back on the amount I'm spending. But it's hard to think of ways to do that without smoking when it's become such a habit.

A short walk, yeah, that's not a bad idea. But I can see myself needing a cigarette after a long delivery, just to take a break. And it's not just about the break, it's also about having something to do with my hands. I'm always eating or smoking when I'm driving, it's a habit.

I could try listening to audiobooks, I've been meaning to try that for ages, or I could use this time to make phone calls to catch up with family or friends instead of just eating and smoking. But the thing is, it's not just about the time, it's about the need for a break from the stress of the road.

Yeah, it's possible, I guess. I mean, I've had moments like that in the past, but they didn't last long. I was trying that once before, to reduce my smoking to 10 a day, but I found myself craving a cigarette during the most stressful parts of my route. It was hard to keep it up.

It's usually the traffic, especially when I'm stuck in a jam. It's frustrating and I feel like I'm wasting time, which makes me anxious. Then there's the long hours alone, it can get monotonous and I find myself reaching for a cigarette just to break the monotony, I guess.

I don't know, it's hard to see it as anything but frustrating. But I guess if I could think of it as a chance to listen to something or catch up on some work, it might make the time go by faster. Or I could try to use that time to relax, breathe, or something. But that's hard to do when I'm sitting still in traffic.

I suppose it couldn't hurt to try. But I've tried deep breathing exercises before, too. It's not like I've never thought to just calm down before. The problem is, when I'm stuck in traffic, my mind starts racing and it's hard to focus on just breathing. But maybe if I tried it a few times, it might become a new habit.

That's not a bad idea. I could try walking a bit, get some fresh air, stretch my legs... It might actually be a decent way to take a break without smoking. And it's not like I'd be wasting time or money, I'd be doing something good for myself instead.

motivation_reason: not wanting to be left out
not wanting to pretend to fit in

session_notes.note: I only smoke at parties or when I'm drinking with friends, maybe 3-4 times a week. It's not like I'm a real smoker. But I've been having asthma attacks more often.

I guess it could help. My doctor said something about how smoke irritates my lungs and makes it harder for me to breathe. I just wish I didn't feel like I have to smoke when I'm out with my friends, you know? It's like, if I don't have a cigarette, I'll stick out as not being like everyone else.

If I could find ways to handle the social pressure, that would be nice. Like, if my friends were supportive when I didn't have a cigarette... or if there were other things we could do together that didn't involve drinking or smoking. That might make it easier for me to say no.
My sister is always trying to get me to quit stuff, but I'm not sure if she'd really understand how hard it is for me. I don't want to be one of those people who can't even stop smoking, you know?

I guess I could show her my asthma doctor's notes, maybe she'll see that it's not just about me being weak or anything. And I could tell her how it would feel to be able to hang out with her and the rest of our friends without feeling like I'm left out or have to pretend to fit in.

I don't know... maybe just say something like, "Hey, I've been thinking a lot about my asthma and I'm getting really worried about it. I was thinking about quitting smoking, but I don't know if I can do it."

I think the biggest challenge will be her not taking it seriously. Like, she'll think I'm just being dramatic or trying to quit cold turkey, but she won't really understand how hard it is. I don't want her to say something like, "Oh, you can just quit anytime you want," because that's just not true.
i have been smoking for 10 years and my doctor says i should quit
no i know quitting will make me feel better
That's what worries me. I smoke a lot when I'm stressed at work and after meals. I don't know what to do in those moments
When I'm stressed I just need something to do with my hands and the break outside helps me reset. It's become my way of coping
I guess the doctor scared me enough to take it seriously. I'll try the gum idea when I feel the urge
my friends smoke and i kind of picked it up 3 years ago. i dont think im addicted or anything. i can stop whenever i want.
No never tried. I smoke maybe 4-5 a day, only when I'm with friends or when I'm bored and stressed
I don't know, everyone just smokes and I join in. It feels weird to say no. Anyway what can I do to stop craving it when I'm bored?
Sorry I meant I don't know how to say no to friends. What can I do when I feel bored and want to smoke
